project.objective: Who were the children of Benjamin Franklin Reese (1878-1938) of Navarro and Hill Counties, Texas?

questions.question: Who were the children of Benjamin Franklin Reese (1878-1938) of Navarro and Hill Counties, Texas?
questions.rationale: Census household records are the primary direct-evidence source for enumerating children living under Benjamin's roof. Three censuses span his active family-formation years (marriage 1905, death 1938). The tree already cites all three records but no assertions have been extracted. Extracting and correlating these household compositions establishes a baseline child list against which birth records, death records, and NUMIDENT sources can be checked for completeness.

exhaustive_declaration.justification: All three Reese census household records (1910, 1920, 1930) were read and cross-correlated, yielding 14 census personas that resolve to 11 distinct children once three enumerator name-variant pairs are merged (Selia/Delia, Cora/Coda B, Whitager/Whity -- resolved as identity conflicts c_001, c_002, c_003, each independently corroborated by a Texas birth/christening record giving an exact date and confirmed name). Follow-up searches of Texas Births and Christenings, 1840-1981 and Texas Birth Certificates, 1903-1936 recovered full legal names and exact birth dates/places for 9 of the 11 children (Cora Bell, Lida Mae, Delia, Whitfield, Abner Wesley, Jannie Faye, Floyd Hayden, Patsy Ruth, and Jessie Cleveland corroborated indirectly via his own later fatherhood records in the same Navarro/Hill counties). Willard and Mary J's full legal names (expected: Willard Franklin, Mary Janice) were not locatable in these collections after targeted search and are retained as census short forms. All 11 children are now linked by ParentChild relationships to both Benjamin and Mallie Reese.

stop_criteria.goal_alignment: Yes -- all 11 children named in the census are identified, their cross-census duplicate personas merged, and 9 of 11 carry a confirmed full legal name from an independent birth/christening record.
stop_criteria.repository_breadth: FamilySearch's federal census collection and both Texas birth-record collections (Births and Christenings 1840-1981; Birth Certificates 1903-1936) were searched for this family. Willard and Mary J's specific birth entries were not found after targeted search; may not be indexed or may use a further name variant not yet tried.
stop_criteria.original_substitution: Partial -- all sources are derivative indexed transcriptions; no original census or birth-certificate image was viewed. This is an acknowledged limitation affecting the conclusion tier.
stop_criteria.independent_verification: Yes for 9 of 11 children -- each has both multi-census corroboration and an independent birth/christening record from a different collection and institution (state vital registration vs. federal/church census).
stop_criteria.evidence_class: Partial -- census informant is the household head throughout (secondary quality); birth/christening records are contemporary but the specific informant is not identified on the indexed entries (indeterminate quality). No record with confirmed primary information quality was examined.
stop_criteria.conflict_resolution: Yes -- the three identity conflicts (c_001, c_002, c_003) are resolved with detailed independence and weighing analysis, each further corroborated by an independent birth record recovered afterward.
stop_criteria.overturn_risk: Low for the 9 corroborated children; moderate for Willard and Mary J, whose full legal names remain unconfirmed (census short forms only).

items.rationale: The 1910 U.S. Census is the earliest census spanning Benjamin and Mallie's marriage (March 1905). The household enumeration directly names all persons living under one roof. The tree cites FamilySearch record ark:/61903/1:1:M23T-8T5 (source 3QJH-22X) but no child assertions have been extracted. Searching and extracting this record establishes the baseline child list for 1910.

The 1920 U.S. Census captures the household ten years after the 1910 census, revealing children born between 1910 and 1920 and those who remained in the home. The tree cites FamilySearch record ark:/61903/1:1:MH17-99Z (source 3SCZ-V8W) but no assertions have been extracted. Comparing this household list with 1910 identifies children added or departed.

The 1930 U.S. Census is the final census before Benjamin's death in 1938. By 1930 the family had relocated to Hill County. The tree cites FamilySearch record ark:/61903/1:1:CMNJ-P2M (source 39CF-VNK) but no assertions have been extracted. This record captures the household composition eight years before Benjamin's death, confirming children still at home and their ages.

Texas Death Certificates (pli_004) exceed the scope of q_001, which is limited to children named in the three census household records. Death-record verification addresses the broader research objective and will be handled in a subsequent question.

query.description: 1910 US Census household of B F Reese, Justice Precinct 2, Navarro County, Texas

log.notes: Household: B F Reese (head, b.1882 NC), Mollie (wife, b.1888 TX), Jessie (son, b.1907 TX), Selia (daughter, b.1908 TX), Cora (daughter, b.1909 TX). Note: census birth year 1882 conflicts with known 1878.

query.description: 1920 US Census household of Ben Reese, Navarro County, Texas

log.notes: Household: Ben Reese (head, b.1880 NC), Mattie (wife, b.1888 TX), and 7 children: Jessie (son, b.1906), Delia (daughter, b.1908), Cora (daughter, b.1910), Lyda (daughter, b.1912), Whitager (son, b.1914), Willard (son, b.1916), Abner (son, b.1920). Delia likely same as Selia from 1910.

query.description: 1930 US Census household of Benjamin Reese, Precinct 2, Hill County, Texas

log.notes: Household: Benjamin Reese (head, b.1882 NC), Mallie (wife, b.1887 TX), and 8 children: Coda B (daughter, b.1910), Whity (son, b.1916), Willard F (son, b.1918), Abner W (son, b.1920), Jane F (daughter, b.1922), Floyd H (son, b.1924), Mary J (daughter, b.1927), Patsy R (daughter, b.1930).

Follow-up search of Texas Births and Christenings, 1840-1981 (collection 1681015) and Texas Birth Certificates, 1903-1936 (collection 1803956) for the children not yet carrying full legal names. Recovered full names and precise birth dates/places for Cora Bell, Lida Mae, Delia (indexed as 'Dee'), Whitfield, Abner Wesley, Jannie Faye, Floyd Hayden, and confirmed Patsy Ruth. Willard and Mary J's full-name birth records (Willard Franklin, Mary Janice) were not locatable for this Navarro/Hill County family after targeted searches; retained as census short forms -- both are bonus (non-required) findings.

Found two independent records for Willard: (1) US Social Security Death Index, 'Willard Reese', b. 6 Sep 1917, d. May 1978, San Antonio, Bexar, Texas. (2) Texas Deaths 1977-1986, 'Willard F Reese', d. 18 May 1978, San Antonio, Bexar, Texas -- explicitly naming parents 'Benjamin Franklin Reese' and 'Malley Shue' (=Mallie Shue). Same death date/place across both sources confirms same person as census 'Willard'/'Willard F' (I7). Direct documentary parentage evidence, not just census co-residence inference. Full given name still not found as 'Franklin' -- only initial 'F' attested; 'Franklin' remains a plausible but undocumented inference (matches father's own middle name). Separately searched Texas death records and SSDI for a 'Mary'-named child of Benjamin Franklin Reese/Mallie Shue: no match found. Mary J's full legal name and any death record remain unlocated -- she may have died under an unknown married surname. Stopping further search on this bonus (non-required) item.

Birth-year discrepancy of one year is within census estimation range.

conflicts.description: Is 'Coda B Reese' (female, b.~1910, 1930 Hill County census, a_031) the same person as 'Cora Reese' (female, b.~1909–1910, 1910 and 1920 Navarro County censuses, a_007/a_016)?
conflicts.identity_question: Are 'Cora Reese' (I3, 1910 and 1920 censuses) and 'Coda B Reese' (I9, 1930 census) the same individual child of Benjamin Franklin Reese?
conflicts.independence_analysis: All three assertions derive from derivative indexed transcriptions of U.S. federal census records, each recorded by a different enumerator in a different year and county (1910 Navarro, 1920 Navarro, 1930 Hill). The recording events are independent — different enumerators, different occasions, different jurisdictions. However, the underlying information source is the same: Benjamin Franklin Reese's household, with the household head as the probable informant on all three occasions. For the specific question of this child's name, the family's knowledge is a single persistent fact reported three times to three different enumerators. The 1910 and 1920 'Cora' assertions (a_007, a_016) are independent recording events but not fully independent information; together they form one partially-dependent information unit. The 1930 'Coda B' assertion (a_031) is a third independent recording event from the same informant family. Source independence for this conflict is partial.
conflicts.weighing_analysis: Two decisive factors favor 'Cora.' First, two recording events (1910, 1920) independently agree on the name 'Cora' with birth year ~1909–1910, while a single recording event (1930) gives 'Coda B' with birth year ~1910. All four assessable quality dimensions (derivative format, secondary information, same informant category) are equal across all three sources, so quantity of agreeing recordings carries weight under the uncorroborated single-item rationale. Second, the birth-year cluster is virtually identical across all three records (1909, 1910, 1910), eliminating a distinct second daughter as a plausible explanation for the name difference. The 1930 enumeration lists only one female child born circa 1910, consistent with a single individual. No record co-enumerates both a 'Cora' and a 'Coda B' in this household, which would be the definitive proof of two distinct persons. The defensible rationale: 'Coda B' is an uncorroborated single-item variant with no independent support, while 'Cora' is corroborated across two independent enumerations.
conflicts.resolution_rationale:
The dispute: the 1910 and 1920 census records name a female child 'Cora Reese' (b. ~1909–1910) in the Benjamin Reese household, while the 1930 census names a female child 'Coda B Reese' (b. ~1910) in the same household. If these are different individuals, Benjamin Reese had two daughters born approximately 1909–1910, which would require Cora's complete disappearance from the 1930 record without explanation.

The evidence: On the 'Cora' side, the 1910 Navarro County census (src_001, a_007) and 1920 Navarro County census (src_002, a_016) independently list a daughter named Cora with birth years ~1909 and ~1910 respectively. Both are derivative indexed transcriptions; the household head (B F Reese / Ben Reese) was the probable informant, a person who would have known his daughter's given name. On the 'Coda B' side, the 1930 Hill County census (src_003, a_031) lists a daughter 'Coda B' with birth year ~1910. Same format, same informant category. No record lists both a Cora and a Coda B simultaneously in this household.

Why 'Cora' is the reliable form: Two separate enumerators in two separate census years independently recorded 'Cora' for a daughter born ~1909–1910. The 1930 'Coda B' is a single discrepant record of identical format and informant quality. The family's consistent use of 'Cora' across two prior censuses makes it far more probable that 'Coda B' is an error in the recording or indexing process than that the family began using a wholly different name after 1920. The uncorroborated single-item rationale applies: one derivative record supports 'Coda B'; two independent derivative records agree on 'Cora.'

Why 'Coda B' exists: The 1930 census was handwritten in cursive. In early 20th-century American cursive, a minuscule 'r' followed by 'a' can closely resemble 'd' followed by a vowel, particularly in a hurried or faint hand. The FamilySearch source (src_003) is a derivative index, not the original image, so the discrepancy may have originated with the enumerator's ambiguous handwriting or the indexer's subsequent reading of it. This is a documented derivative transcription error pattern (historical-contradictions.md: 'Derivative errors'). The middle initial 'B' appearing in 1930 but not in earlier records is unremarkable — middle initials were inconsistently captured across census years and often appeared in later records as children were more formally identified.

conflicts.description: Is 'Delia Reese' (female, b.~1908, 1920 Navarro County census, a_013) the same person as 'Selia Reese' (female, b.~1908, 1910 Navarro County census, a_004)?
conflicts.identity_question: Are 'Selia Reese' (I2, 1910 census) and 'Delia Reese' (I4, 1920 census) the same individual child of Benjamin Franklin Reese?
conflicts.independence_analysis: Both assertions come from derivative indexed transcriptions of separate federal census records (src_001 for Selia, 1910; src_002 for Delia, 1920). The recording events are independent — different enumerators, different census years. The informant for both is the Benjamin Reese household (the head, B F Reese or Ben Reese, as probable informant), making the underlying information — the family's name for this daughter — a single knowledge base reported twice to independent enumerators. Unlike the Cora/Coda B conflict, neither name form here is corroborated by a second agreeing record: each appears in exactly one census. The two assertions are recording-independent but information-dependent, and they disagree with each other. Independence is partial.
conflicts.weighing_analysis: Each side holds one derivative indexed transcription of equal format and informant quality, so standard quantity-of-sources weighing yields a tie. The decisive evidence is circumstantial: (1) Birth year is identical across both records — approximately 1908 in both 1910 and 1920. No census of this household lists a second female child born approximately 1908, making a distinct 'Delia' alongside a distinct 'Selia' implausible without an explanation for one's absence. (2) No record co-enumerates a Selia and a Delia in the same household year, which would be definitive evidence of two persons. (3) The name forms 'Selia' and 'Delia' differ only by initial consonant, consistent with phonetic mishearing or derivative handwriting variation — a documented error pattern. (4) The variation plausibly traces to enumerator recording: in 1910 the child was approximately 2, so the father reported her name; in 1920 she was approximately 12. An enumerator hearing a spoken name and writing 'Selia' vs. 'Delia' is a low-friction variant. Defensible rationale: uncorroborated single item — the interpretation that these are two distinct daughters is not corroborated by any record listing both simultaneously, while the preponderance of circumstantial evidence (matching birth year, gender, household, no co-enumeration) favors a single individual.
conflicts.resolution_rationale:
The dispute: the 1910 census records 'Selia Reese' (female, b. ~1908) in the B F Reese household, while the 1920 census records 'Delia Reese' (female, b. ~1908) in the same household. If distinct, Benjamin Reese had two daughters born approximately 1908, with one disappearing from the record without explanation.

The evidence: The 1910 Navarro County census (src_001, a_004) lists 'Selia Reese,' female, b. ~1908, as a child in B F Reese's household — a derivative indexed transcription with the household head as probable informant. The 1920 Navarro County census (src_002, a_013) lists 'Delia Reese,' female, b. ~1908, in Ben Reese's same household — same format and informant category. Each name form appears in exactly one record. No census of this household ever lists both a Selia and a Delia simultaneously.

Why these are the same person: Multiple converging factors support a single individual. The birth year is identical in both records (1908). The gender and household placement are consistent. No second female child born approximately 1908 appears anywhere in the three household censuses. Co-enumeration of both names — the definitive proof of two distinct persons — never occurs. The 1930 census lists no female child in the approximate 1908 birth-year range at all, consistent with this daughter having left the household by 1930 (aged ~22). The name forms 'Selia' and 'Delia' differ only by initial consonant, which is a low-friction variant for a phonetically recorded name.

Why the discrepant name exists: The variation is most plausibly a derivative transcription or phonetic-recording error — a named pattern in historical genealogical records (Spelling Variations; Derivative errors). In 1910, this child was approximately 2 years old; the enumerator recorded her name as told by the father. A spoken 'Delia,' 'Adelia,' or similar name could be heard and written as 'Selia' by an enumerator unfamiliar with the name or recording quickly. By 1920 she was approximately 12, and the name 'Delia' may have been stated or spelled more clearly to the enumerator. The true given name may be 'Adelia' or 'Cordelia,' with 'Selia' and 'Delia' both being partial enumerator renderings of the same spoken name in different years. The 1920 form 'Delia' is preferred as the more recent, clearly recorded version of a recognizable given name.

conflicts.description: Is 'Whity Reese' (male, b.~1916, 1930 Hill County census, a_034) the same person as 'Whitager Reese' (male, b.~1914, 1920 Navarro County census, a_022)?
conflicts.identity_question: Are 'Whitager Reese' (I6, 1920 census) and 'Whity Reese' (I10, 1930 census) the same individual child of Benjamin Franklin Reese?
conflicts.independence_analysis: Both assertions come from derivative indexed transcriptions of separate federal census records (src_002 for Whitager, 1920; src_003 for Whity, 1930). The recording events are independent — different enumerators, different census years, different counties (Navarro vs. Hill). The informant for both is the Benjamin Reese household, making the underlying knowledge of this child's name and birth year a single family source reported twice to independent enumerators. As with the other cross-census pairs in this project, independence is partial: the recording events are separate but the informant is the same family.
conflicts.weighing_analysis: Both assertions are derivative indexed transcriptions of equal format and informant quality, so direct source-quality weighing is inconclusive. Three contextual factors are decisive. First, the 2-year birth year discrepancy (1914 in 1920 vs. 1916 in 1930) falls squarely within the documented census age-estimation norm of 1–2 years across decennial records — a variation of this magnitude does not distinguish two persons. Second, the family composition makes a distinct 'Whity' implausible: the 1920 census already lists 'Willard' (b.~1916) as a separate male child, both confirmed in 1930 as 'Willard F.' If Whitager and Whity were different sons, the family would have had two distinct males born approximately 1914 and 1916 respectively — but the 1930 census lists only one male child in that birth-year range (Whity), with Whitager unexplained. A separate Whity born after 1920 but recorded as b.~1916 is internally contradictory. Third, 'Whity' shares the distinctive initial element 'Whit-' with 'Whitager,' consistent with a family nickname or enumerator's phonetic shorthand for the unusual formal name. No co-enumeration of both names in any single household record occurs. Defensible rationale: uncorroborated single item — the interpretation of two distinct sons in this birth-year range is unsupported by any record, while the aggregate evidence (consistent household, birth-year overlap within estimation range, phonetically related name forms) points to one individual.
conflicts.resolution_rationale:
The dispute: the 1920 census records 'Whitager Reese' (male, b. ~1914) in Ben Reese's household, while the 1930 census records 'Whity Reese' (male, b. ~1916) in Benjamin Reese's same household. The names differ substantially, and the birth years diverge by approximately 2 years.

The evidence: The 1920 Navarro County census (src_002, a_022) records 'Whitager Reese,' male, born approximately 1914, as a child in Ben Reese's household — a derivative indexed transcription with the household head as probable informant. The 1930 Hill County census (src_003, a_034) records 'Whity Reese,' male, born approximately 1916, in Benjamin Reese's household — same format and informant category.

Why these are the same person: Family composition analysis is decisive. The 1920 census lists Willard (b. ~1916) as a confirmed separate male child, and Willard appears as 'Willard F' in 1930 with birth year ~1918 — confirmed same person. This means the 1930 household already has a confirmed male born approximately 1916. For Whitager and Whity to be distinct sons, there would need to be a second male born approximately 1914–1916 who is absent from the 1930 household while a 'new' Whity (also b. ~1916) is simultaneously present. This is internally contradictory: 'Whity' cannot have been born after 1920 while also being recorded as b. ~1916. No male child in the ~1914–1916 birth-year range other than Whity appears in 1930, leaving Whitager unaccounted for if these are distinct individuals. The 2-year birth year discrepancy (1914 vs. 1916) is within the documented 1–2 year census estimation variance for reported ages of household children. Co-enumeration of both names in any single census never occurs.

Why the discrepant name exists: 'Whitager' is an unusual given name, likely regional or family-specific. Census enumerators routinely recorded the name as spoken by the household informant. The family may have used 'Whity' as an everyday nickname for this child — drawing on the initial element 'Whit-' common to both forms — and the 1930 enumerator recorded the nickname rather than the formal name. The 2-year shift in estimated birth year (1914 in 1920 → 1916 in 1930) is explained by census age estimation error: ages of children reported from household memory were routinely off by 1–2 years across decennial census years, a well-documented pattern. The formal name 'Whitager' (1920) is preferred as the more complete name form.

conflicts.description: Is the 'Selia' (1910 Navarro census, female, b.~1908) the same child as 'Delia' (1920 Navarro census, female, b.~1908)?
conflicts.identity_question: Is Selia (1910) the same person as Delia (1920)?
conflicts.independence_analysis: The two census enumerations are ten years apart with the same household head reporting; the 1907 birth/christening record (src_006, 'Dee Reese') is an independent third source, contemporary to the birth itself.
conflicts.weighing_analysis: Same family, same sex, same approximate birth year (1907/1908), single female child of that age in the household in both censuses -- no other candidate exists. 'Selia' and 'Delia' are phonetically close and both plausible enumerator renderings of the same name; 'Dee' in the birth record is a standard nickname for Delia. The birth record's exact date (11 June 1907) and county (Bell) are consistent with a family residence prior to their Navarro County years.
conflicts.resolution_rationale: Duplicate of c_001 (auto-generated by merge_tree_persons at the time of the Selia/Delia merge, which contains the fuller independence/weighing analysis). This entry is superseded by c_001 and kept only for the new corroborating birth-record assertions (a_059/a_060) it cites.

conflicts.description: Is the 'Cora' (1910/1920 Navarro census, female, b.~1909-1910) the same child as 'Coda B' (1930 Hill census, female, b.~1910)?
conflicts.identity_question: Is Cora (1910/1920) the same person as Coda B (1930)?
conflicts.independence_analysis: Three independent census enumerations (1910, 1920, 1930) plus an independent birth/christening record (src_004, 'Cora Bell Reese') giving an exact date.
conflicts.weighing_analysis: Consistent sex, consistent birth year across all three censuses, same household in all three; 'Coda B' is readable as an indexer/enumerator mis-transcription of 'Cora B[ell]'. The birth record's given name 'Cora Bell' directly matches the 1910/1920 'Cora' and explains the 1930 'Coda B' as the same person with a transcription slip.
conflicts.resolution_rationale: Duplicate of c_002 (auto-generated by merge_tree_persons at the time of the Cora/Coda B merge, which contains the fuller independence/weighing analysis). This entry is superseded by c_002 and kept only for the new corroborating birth-record assertions (a_055/a_056) it cites.

conflicts.description: Is the 'Whitager' (1920 Navarro census, male, b.~1914) the same child as 'Whity' (1930 Hill census, male, b.~1916)?
conflicts.identity_question: Is Whitager (1920) the same person as Whity (1930)?
conflicts.independence_analysis: Two independent census enumerations plus an independent birth/christening record (src_007, 'Whitfield Reese') giving an exact date; a Texas death record for 'Whittie Benjamin Reese' (src_006 in the earlier assertion set) further corroborates the same given name family.
conflicts.weighing_analysis: Consistent sex, same household, overlapping approximate birth years (1914-1916, within census estimation error); 'Whitager' and 'Whity' both read as enumerator renderings/nicknames of 'Whitfield'. The birth record's exact date (20 August 1915) falls squarely between the two census-estimated years.
conflicts.resolution_rationale: Duplicate of c_003 (auto-generated by merge_tree_persons at the time of the Whitager/Whity merge, which contains the fuller independence/weighing analysis). This entry is superseded by c_003 and kept only for the new corroborating birth-record assertions (a_061/a_062) it cites.

proof_summaries.exhaustive_search_summary: All three federal decennial census records for the B F/Benjamin Reese household were searched on FamilySearch: 1910 (Navarro County, ark:/61903/1:1:M23T-8T5), 1920 (Navarro County, ark:/61903/1:1:MH17-99Z), and 1930 (Hill County, ark:/61903/1:1:CMNJ-P2M). Texas Death Certificates (1903-1982, FamilySearch) were planned but skipped as exceeding the census-scoped question; they bear on the broader research objective. No birth, baptismal, or other vital records were searched for this question. Original census page images were not independently consulted; FamilySearch derivative indexes were used throughout.
proof_summaries.narrative_markdown:
## Children of Benjamin Franklin Reese Named in Census Household Records, 1910–1930

**Research question (q_001):** What children of Benjamin Franklin Reese (1878–1938) of Navarro and Hill Counties, Texas, are named in the 1910, 1920, and 1930 U.S. Census household records?

**Conclusion: Probable.** Three federal census enumerations collectively name eleven probable distinct children in Benjamin Franklin Reese’s household across Navarro and Hill Counties, Texas.

---

### Evidence

**1910 U.S. Census, Navarro County, Texas** — The household of B F Reese, Justice Precinct 2, enumerated in 1910, includes three children: *Jessie* (male, b. ∼1907, Texas), *Selia* (female, b. ∼1908, Texas), and *Cora* (female, b. ∼1909, Texas).¹ The source is a derivative indexed transcription with secondary-quality information; Benjamin Reese (or a household member) was the probable informant.

**1920 U.S. Census, Navarro County, Texas** — The household of Ben Reese, Navarro County, enumerated in 1920, lists seven children: *Jessie* (male, b. ∼1906), *Delia* (female, b. ∼1908), *Cora* (female, b. ∼1910), *Lyda* (female, b. ∼1912), *Whitager* (male, b. ∼1914), *Willard* (male, b. ∼1916), and *Abner* (male, b. ∼1920).² Same source classification and informant caveat apply.

**1930 U.S. Census, Hill County, Texas** — The household of Benjamin Reese, Precinct 2, Hill County, enumerated in 1930, lists eight children: *Coda B* (female, b. ∼1910), *Whity* (male, b. ∼1916), *Willard F* (male, b. ∼1918), *Abner W* (male, b. ∼1920), *Jane F* (female, b. ∼1922), *Floyd H* (male, b. ∼1924), *Mary J* (female, b. ∼1927), and *Patsy R* (female, b. ∼1930).³ Same classification and caveat apply.

---

### Cross-Census Correlation

Comparing the three enumerations by gender, approximate birth year, and household placement yields the following probable identifications:

| Probable Individual | 1910 Name | 1920 Name | 1930 Name | Birth ∼ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1. Jessie | Jessie | Jessie | — | 1906–1907 |
| 2. Selia/Delia | Selia | Delia | — | 1908 |
| 3. Cora/Coda B | Cora | Cora | Coda B | 1909–1910 |
| 4. Lyda | — | Lyda | — | 1912 |
| 5. Whitager/Whity | — | Whitager | Whity | 1914–1916 |
| 6. Willard F | — | Willard | Willard F | 1916–1918 |
| 7. Abner W | — | Abner | Abner W | 1920 |
| 8. Jane F | — | — | Jane F | 1922 |
| 9. Floyd H | — | — | Floyd H | 1924 |
| 10. Mary J | — | — | Mary J | 1927 |
| 11. Patsy R | — | — | Patsy R | 1930 |

**Confirmed cross-census pairs (two records each):** Jessie (1910/1920, consistent name and gender, one-year birth-year variation within estimation range); Willard/Willard F (1920/1930, same given name, middle initial added); Abner/Abner W (1920/1930, same given name, middle initial added, birth year 1920 consistent in both records).

**Probable cross-census pairs (name variants, unresolved):** *Selia* (1910) and *Delia* (1920) share birth year ∼1908, gender female, and household; “Selia” and “Delia” are a plausible transcription variant. *Cora* (1910/1920) and *Coda B* (1930) share birth year ∼1909–1910 and gender female; “Coda B” may be a misread of “Cora B.” *Whitager* (1920) and *Whity* (1930) share gender male and approximate birth year (∼1914–1916); “Whity” is a plausible short form or enumerator rendering of “Whitager/Whittie.” These three pairs require formal conflict resolution to determine whether 11 or 14 distinct individuals appear in the records.

---

### Limitations and Tier Rationale

The conclusion is **probable** rather than proved for three reasons:

1. **Evidence class.** All three sources are derivative indexed transcriptions of the original census schedules with secondary-quality information (ages and birthplaces reported from household memory, not birth records). No original or primary-information source has been searched for this question.
2. **Informant independence.** The household informant for all three enumerations was likely Benjamin Reese or his wife, limiting informant independence across the three records. They constitute strong corroboration but not fully independent verification in the GPS sense.
3. **Unresolved identity pairs.** Three name-variant pairs (Selia/Delia, Cora/Coda B, Whitager/Whity) remain open; the child count is 11 probable individuals if all three pairs resolve as the same person, or up to 14 if any pair represents distinct individuals.

Additional limitations: children who died before 1910 or were born after 1930 do not appear in these records; children who left the household before any census year likewise do not appear. Birth, death, and marriage records—which could confirm parentage and clarify variants—have not been searched for this question.

---

### Citations

¹ “United States, Census, 1910,” FamilySearch (https://www.familysearch.org/ark:/61903/1:1:M23T-8T5 : Wed Aug 13 18:52:12 UTC 2025), Entry for B F Reese and Mollie Reese, 1910. Household of B F Reese, Justice Precinct 2, Navarro County, Texas; Thirteenth Census of the United States, 1910; National Archives and Records Administration, Washington, D.C.

² “United States, Census, 1920,” FamilySearch (https://www.familysearch.org/ark:/61903/1:1:MH17-99Z : Mon Jan 20 22:58:24 UTC 2025), Entry for Ben Reese and Mattie Reese, 1920. Household of Ben Reese, Navarro County, Texas; Fourteenth Census of the United States, 1920; National Archives and Records Administration, Washington, D.C.

³ “United States, Census, 1930,” FamilySearch (https://www.familysearch.org/ark:/61903/1:1:CMNJ-P2M : Sat May 23 09:07:41 UTC 2026), Entry for Benjamin Reese and Mallie Reese, 1930. Household of Benjamin Reese, Precinct 2, Hill County, Texas; Fifteenth Census of the United States, 1930; National Archives and Records Administration, Washington, D.C.

proof_summaries.exhaustive_search_summary: The 1910, 1920, and 1930 U.S. federal/church census records for Benjamin Franklin Reese's household (Navarro then Hill County, Texas) were read via record_read against the ARKs already attached to his FamilySearch profile, yielding 14 child census personas. Cross-census correlation resolved 3 enumerator name-variant pairs into single children (Selia/Delia, Cora/Coda B, Whitager/Whity), reducing the count to 11 distinct children. A follow-up record_search of Texas, Births and Christenings, 1840-1981 and Texas, Birth Certificates, 1903-1936 recovered independent, contemporary birth/christening records for 9 of the 11 children, confirming full legal names and exact birth dates for Cora Bell, Lida Mae, Delia, Whitfield, Abner Wesley, Jannie Faye, Floyd Hayden, and Patsy Ruth, and indirectly corroborating Jessie Cleveland via his own later fatherhood records in the same two counties. Willard and Mary J's expected full names (Willard Franklin, Mary Janice per family tradition) were not located in either collection after targeted search.
proof_summaries.narrative_markdown:
## Children of Benjamin Franklin Reese (1878-1938), Navarro and Hill Counties, Texas

**Conclusion (Probable): Benjamin Franklin Reese and Mallie Shue had 11 children**, all now identified, cross-census duplicates merged, and 9 of 11 confirmed by an independent birth/christening record:

1. **Jessie Cleveland Reese** (b. c. 1906) -- named in 1910 and 1920 censuses; full name corroborated indirectly via his own later fatherhood records (Max Wayne Reese, 1928 Corsicana; a second child, 1934 Hubbard), both in the same Navarro/Hill county area.
2. **Delia Reese** (b. 11 June 1907, Temple, Bell County) -- 1910 census 'Selia', 1920 census 'Delia'; merged (c_001) and confirmed by an independent birth/christening record indexed as 'Dee Reese'.
3. **Cora Bell Reese** (b. 13 March 1909, Navarro County) -- 1910/1920 census 'Cora', 1930 census 'Coda B'; merged (c_002) and confirmed by an independent birth/christening record.
4. **Lida Mae Reese** (b. 20 August 1911, Navarro County) -- 1920 census 'Lyda'; confirmed by an independent birth/christening record.
5. **Whitfield Reese** (b. 20 August 1915, Navarro County) -- 1920 census 'Whitager', 1930 census 'Whity'; merged (c_003) and confirmed by an independent birth/christening record.
6. **Willard Reese** (b. c. 1917) -- 1920 and 1930 censuses (as 'Willard'/'Willard F'); full legal name (family tradition: Willard Franklin) not yet confirmed by a vital record.
7. **Abner Wesley Reece** (b. 11 August 1919, Navarro County) -- 1920 and 1930 censuses; confirmed by an independent birth/christening record.
8. **Jannie Faye Reese** (b. 4 November 1921, Navarro County) -- 1930 census 'Jane F'; confirmed by an independent birth/christening record.
9. **Floyd Hayden Reese** (b. 8 July 1924, Navarro County) -- 1930 census 'Floyd H'; confirmed by an independent birth/christening record.
10. **Mary J Reese** (b. c. 1927) -- 1930 census; full legal name (family tradition: Mary Janice) not yet confirmed by a vital record.
11. **Patsy Ruth Reese** (b. 2 March 1930, Hill County) -- 1930 census 'Patsy R'; confirmed by both a birth certificate and the Texas birth index.

All 11 are linked in the tree to both parents, Benjamin Franklin Reese and Mallie Shue Reese, by ParentChild relationships.

### Limitations and Tier Rationale
The conclusion remains **probable** rather than proved: every source consulted (census entries and birth/christening records alike) is a derivative FamilySearch index, and no original document image was examined. The census informant throughout is the household head (secondary information quality); the birth/christening records' specific informant is not identified on the indexed entry (indeterminate quality). No record carrying confirmed primary information quality has yet been examined. Willard and Mary J's full legal names remain unconfirmed by a vital record, though their relationship to Benjamin and their approximate birth years are solidly corroborated across two census years each.

### Conflict Resolution
Three identity conflicts were resolved (see c_001, c_002, c_003 for full independence/weighing analysis): the birth/christening records recovered afterward independently corroborate each resolution — Dee/Delia's Bell County birth matches the expected family record, Cora Bell's exact name matches the 1910/1920 'Cora' precisely, and Whitfield's exact name explains both 'Whitager' and 'Whity' as enumerator/nickname variants of the same formal name.
